title: Negative Differential Resistance and Ultra-High TMR in Altermagnetic Tunnel Junctions
authors: Sajjan Sheoran, Luke Keenan, Declan Nell, Stefano Sanvito
abstract: Altermagnets can replace ferromagnets in tunnel junctions, yielding large tunneling magnetoresistance, ultrafast switching, and low-power functionality. While most studies explore the linear-response regime, interesting features emerge at finite bias, where the peculiar electronic structure of altermagnets gives rise to complex non-linear behaviour. Using non-equilibrium Green's functions implemented with density functional theory, we predict that a large low-bias negative differential resistance can be observed in an altermagnetic tunnel junction. Our proposed junction incorporates the orbital-ordered altermagnet KV2Se2O, whose quasi-2D Fermi surface plays a crucial role in realizing the negative differential resistance. Upon the application of a finite bias voltage, the current in the parallel configuration first increases sharply and then decreases, to be almost completely suppressed at around 0.14 V. At the same time, the antiparallel configuration displays a monotonic current-voltage curve. This behaviour, in addition to the negative differential resistance, supports a large tunneling magnetoresistance with sign inversion at 0.13 V. Our results suggest that altermagnetic tunnel junctions can be used as components in applications requiring strong non-linear response at low bias.
summary: This paper predicts that altermagnetic tunnel junctions can exhibit significant non-linear transport effects, such as negative differential resistance and TMR sign inversion, at low bias voltages. These effects arise from the unique momentum-space electronic structure of altermagnets. This discovery suggests potential applications for altermagnetic components in high-performance, low-power spintronic devices.
main_problem: Investigating the non-linear transport properties of altermagnetic tunnel junctions (AMTJs) at finite bias, specifically looking for negative differential resistance (NDR) and ultra-high tunneling magnetoresistance (TMR).
main_result: The study predicts a Lambda-type negative differential resistance and a TMR sign inversion at approximately 0.13 V, driven by the momentum-space mismatch of spin-polarized Fermi surfaces in the parallel configuration.
method: Non-equilibrium Green's functions (NEGF) implemented with Density Functional Theory (DFT) using the Smeagol package and Siesta engine.
model_or_system: An altermagnetic tunnel junction consisting of KV2Se2O electrodes separated by a 5-monolayer MgO barrier. The system utilizes the orbital-ordered, d-wave altermagnet KV2Se2O with a quasi-2D Fermi surface.
key_observables: Current-voltage (I-Vb) curves, Tunneling Magnetoresistance (TMR), and k-parallel-resolved transmission T(k_parallel).
important_parameters: Bias voltage (Vb), parallel (P) and antiparallel (AP) magnetic configurations, and the spin-momentum anisotropy parameter (tJ).
main_assumptions: The study focuses on the maximally spin-anisotropic regime and assumes a vertical junction setup with a specific orbital-ordered structure.
figures_summary: The notes do not provide specific figure descriptions, though they mention the presence of k-parallel-resolved transmission and I-V curves.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the concept of altermagnetic tunnel junctions, describes the electronic structure and transport modeling of the KV2Se2O/MgO/KV2Se2O system, presents the results for current-voltage characteristics and TMR, and discusses the physical mechanism of NDR and TMR sign inversion.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Simple model analysis. In panel (a) we illustrate the P configuration of the AMTJ with included the spin-resolved Fermi surfaces described in Eq. (1) (t = tJ = 1 and µ = 0). The red (blue) surfaces are the spin-up (spin-down) Fermi surfaces. In the AP configuration, the Néel vector of one altermagnetic lead is switched relative to the other. Panels (b) and (c) show the k∥-resolved transmission for the P and AP configurations at equilibrium Fermi energy, respectively. In (d) we present the I-V curve for the P and AP configurations for different spectral broadening, Γ.

Figure 2: Zero-bias transport properties of the KV2Se2O|MgO|KV2Se2O junction. (a) the atomistic structure of the scattering region in the P configuration of the Néel vectors. This is connected to two semi-infinite KV2Se2O leads on both sides. The AP configuration is obtained by reversing the Néel vector of the right lead. (b) Spin-polarized band structure and (c) Fermi surface of KV2Se2O. Red surfaces are for spin up, while the blue ones for spin down. (d) Transmission and TMR as functions of energy at zero bias (EF = 0). (e) k∥-resolved transmission for the spin-up and spin-down states at EF in the P configuration. See Fig. S5 in the SI for the k∥-resolved transmission of the AP...

Figure 3: Bias-dependent transmission properties of the KV2Se2O|MgO|KV2Se2O junction. The transmis- sion coefficients for the (a) P and (b) AP configuration are presented as a function of energy for different voltages. In (c) and (d) we show the k∥- and spin-resolved transmission coefficient at EF in the P configu- ration at a finite bias of 0.1 V and 0.2 V, respectively. Note that the heat map is on a logarithmic scale.

Figure 4: (a) I-Vb characteristics of the KV2Se2O|MgO|KV2Se2O junction for the P and AP configurations and associated (b) bias-dependent “optimistic” TMR, calculated as IP−IAP min(IP,IAP) and plotted on a symmetric logarithmic scale.
